coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of molybdenum trioxide/graphite carbon nitride heterojunction modified by biomass carbon dots and its application in photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Fenglin
Tang, Jiaojiao
Wang, Dan
Deng, Caixia
Li, Shuang
Yang, Xiupei
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a hexagonal molybdenum trioxide/graphite carbon nitride heterojunction modified by biomass carbon dots was synthesized by a simple one-pot method and successfully applied to the degradation of methylene blue as a photocatalyst. It was confirmed that under optimal compounding conditions, graphite carbon nitride and hexagonal molybdenum trioxide combined to form a heterojunction, and the coupling interface accelerated the charge transfer efficiency. The modification of supplementary biomass carbon dots can improve the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue. The proposed heterojunction shows better photocatalytic activity than bulk graphite carbon nitride and has good reusability and stability. After irradiation with simulated visible light for 1 h, the degradation rate of methylene blue by the heterojunction reached 67 %, which was 2.7, 2.6 and 5.8 times higher than that of bulk graphite carbon nitride, pure hexagonal molybdenum trioxide and pure carbon dots, respectively.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of molybdenum trioxide/graphite carbon nitride heterojunction modified by biomass carbon dots and its application in photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a hexagonal molybdenum trioxide/graphite carbon nitride heterojunction modified by biomass carbon dots was synthesized by a simple one-pot method and successfully applied to the degradation of methylene blue as a photocatalyst. It was confirmed that under optimal compounding conditions, graphite carbon nitride and hexagonal molybdenum trioxide combined to form a heterojunction, and the coupling interface accelerated the charge transfer efficiency. The modification of supplementary biomass carbon dots can improve the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue. The proposed heterojunction shows better photocatalytic activity than bulk graphite carbon nitride and has good reusability and stability. After irradiation with simulated visible light for 1 h, the degradation rate of methylene blue by the heterojunction reached 67 %, which was 2.7, 2.6 and 5.8 times higher than that of bulk graphite carbon nitride, pure hexagonal molybdenum trioxide and pure carbon dots, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of the printing and dyeing industries, printing and dyeing wastewater poses a potential threat to people's living environments and lives. As a kind of sulfur nitride dye, methylene blue (MB) is widely used in textile printing and dyeing processes [1,2]. It has a great toxic effect on aquatic organisms, so the development of efficient treatment methods for MB in wastewater is particularly important for environmental protection [3,4]. Due to the rapid development of industry today, a large amount of energy on Earth is consumed [5], and the development of new energy has become a key requirement for the development of human society [6]. Photocatalytic oxidation technology is a green, efficient and extremely low energy consumption method to convert light energy into chemical energy, which can be used in environmental purification [7,8]. It is a green and environmentally friendly solution to energy shortages and pollutant treatment [9]. The g-C3N4 semiconductor has undergone excellent development in the field of photocatalysis due to its suitable band gap position (2.70 eV) and effective absorption and utilization of visible light [10–12]. The original massive g-C3N4 has a 2D layered structure similar to graphene [13,14]. The two-dimensional planar layer is connected by the CN bond of the basic structural unit—the tris-triazine pyrimidine unit (heptazine ring)—and the layers are connected by a weaker van der Waals force connection [15]. It exhibits excellent thermal and chemical stability. However, the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers often occurs on a single-phase photocatalyst [16], so researchers have performed various modifications on g-C3N4, such as sensitization doping [17,18] and heterojunction recombination [19,20], which can improve the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 [21]. Different materials have different energy band structures, and the staggered conduction and valence bands form a strong internal electric field between the coupling interfaces [22]. In this way, the plasma effect is used to broaden the photoresponse range and improve the redox ability of the photocatalyst [23] so that the rate of light excitation to generate electron-hole pairs is faster than the rate of charge recombination and consumption, and the photocatalytic reaction rate is improved [24].

Molybdenum trioxide (MoO3) is a typical n-type semiconductor with abundant sources, nontoxicity and good stability [25]. It can be excited by visible light and ultraviolet light to generate electrons to form photogenerated electron-hole pairs [26], which is a highly anticipated potential photocatalyst [27]. h-MoO3 is a hexagonal metastable structure, which is one of the three common structures of MoO3 [28,29]. Compared with the highly stable orthogonal phase (α-MoO3) and another metastable monoclinic phase (β-MoO3), it shows better photoelectrochemical performance [30,31]. However, due to its low light absorption and wide band gap (2.8–3.2 eV), its response to visible light is limited and needs to be modified to improve its photocatalytic performance [32]. The combination of MoO3 and g-C3N4 has been confirmed as a Z-type heterojunction complex [33–35]. The structure of the heterojunction can enrich the photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of the g-C3N4 photocatalyst, while the valence band of MoO3 retains holes. The photoelectric charge can be quickly distributed, and the reduction and oxidation abilities of electron holes can be maintained [34].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based materials composed of spherical carbon particles with a diameter of <10 nm [35]. Due to its unique upconversion fluorescence properties [36], it can convert high-wavelength photons to low-wavelength photons by continuously absorbing multiple low-energy photons [37]. It can also expand the light absorption area, cover visible light to the ultraviolet or near ultraviolet region, and reduce photoinduced electron-hole pair recombination. It improves the utilization of visible light and is an excellent photosensitizer [38]. For heterojunction materials, the electron transfer at the interface between the two materials has a certain limit [39]. Loading the electronic medium between the coupling interfaces is beneficial for improving the catalytic performance of the heterogeneous catalyst [38]. C-dots (CDs) are widely used in the modification of heterojunction catalytic materials. Heterostructures modified by CDs can generate excess light-excited carriers during photocatalysis [40,41].

In this work, the reported method was used to synthesize h-MoO3 by a solution self-assembly method, and biomass CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using aloe as the raw material. The carbon-dot-modified h-MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction (CMOCN) was synthesized by a one-pot method with h-MoO3 and CDs and bulk g-C3N4 (BCN). Through the degradation of the dye MB (10 mg/L) within the same period as the evaluation standard, the preparation method of the h-MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction, the compounding ratio and the amount of CD modification were discussed. The morphology and structure of CMOCN were described by XRD, FT-IR, XPS, etc., and its reusability and stability were discussed through cyclic experiments, which provided a reference for the composite modification of the g-C3N4 heterojunction.

EDTA-2Na, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), C2H6O, and HNO3 (65 %–68 %) were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). C3H6N6, H8MoN2O4, BaSO4 and P-benzoquinone (97 %) were obtained from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and methylene blue was purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Unless otherwise specified, the reagents used in this study were commercial and analytical and did not require further purification.

Bulk g-C3N4 was prepared by a high-temperature, one-step thermal polymerization method. Five grams of uniformly ground white melamine powder was poured into a 50 mL ceramic crucible with a lid, and then the crucible was placed into a muffle furnace, heated to 550 °C at 2 °C/min, and warmed for 4 h. After it was naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained yellow solid was ground evenly with an agate mortar to obtain the original g-C3N4 photocatalyst powder, which was bottled and labeled BCN for later use.

Hexagonal molybdenum trioxide (h-MoO3) was prepared by the synthetic method described in the literature [42]. First, prepare a 0.2 M aqueous solution of ammonium molybdate in 30 mL of deionized water. After stirring at room temperature for 30 min, 10 mL of concentrated nitric acid was slowly added dropwise to the solution and heated at 120 °C for 3 h. After the reaction was completed, the precipitate was washed with deionized water, collected by centrifugation, and dried at 70 °C. A gray–green solid was obtained, which was evenly ground, bottled for later use and labeled h-MoO3.

A simple biomass CD was synthesized using a hydrothermal method [43]. A total of 5.5 g of Aloe vera was washed with ultrapure water. The samples were cut into small particles with scissors and placed into a 50 mL reactor. Then, 27.5 mL of ultrapure water was added, and the mixture was hydrothermally reacted at 180 °C for 11 h. Then, the samples were stored at 4 °C and labeled CDs.

Heterojunction composites are synthesized by three different methods, and the heterojunction composition ratio was optimized. Among them, the fixed two-component mass was 2 g, and the feeding ratio of h-MoO3 is 0 %, 1 %, 3 % and 5 %, respectively. The synthesis methods of heterojunctions are as follows.
(1)
High-temperature calcination after direct mixing [44]. The components in different proportions are mixed uniformly and calcined at a high temperature of 300 °C for 4 h. After the reaction is naturally cooled to room temperature, the product is evenly ground and bottled for later use, which is recorded as MOCN-0mix, MOCN-1mix, MOCN-3mix and MOCN-5mix.
(2)
Ultrasonic dispersion in ethanol [45]. The mixed components were placed in different proportions into 40 mL of ethanol and sonicated for 2 h to thoroughly mix the two components. The samples were carefully stirred at 75 °C to evaporate the ethanol, dried at 120 °C for 12 h, ground evenly and set aside. They were denoted by MOCN-0bf, MOCN-1bf, MOCN-3bf and MOCN-5bf.
(3)
Ultrasonic dispersion in ethanol before calcination [46]. The catalyst was obtained according to method (2) and then transferred to a muffle furnace for high-temperature calcination at 300 °C for 4 h, cooled naturally to room temperature, and ground evenly for later use. Marked as MOCN-0af, MOCN-1af, MOCN-3af and MOCN-5af.

In Method 2.5-(2), on the basis of adding absolute ethanol, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mL of CDs were added, with CD volume ratios of 1.25 %, 2.50 %, 5.00 %, and 7.50 %, respectively [47,48]. The resulting materials were labeled xCMOCN-3 (x represents the added volume of CDs). According to the measured density of CDs (0.97 g/mL), the weight ratio of CD:h-MoO3:g-C3N4 in 1CMOCN-3 is 0.97:3:100.

The morphology and microstructure were obtained on a Carl Zeiss Gemini 500 (Carl Zelss, Shanghai) scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were monitored on a Rigaku Dmax/Ultima IV diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the photocatalyst were characterized using ASAP 2020 (USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken on a Thermo Fischer, ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS, Shimazu UV-3600) was used to analyze the light absorption performance of the photocatalyst. The recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes in the photocatalyst was determined by a fluorescence spectrometer (PL, Hitachi F-4600) at room temperature. The photoelectrochemical performance was obtained using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. Using a three-electrode system, Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode, a Pt electrode was used as the counter electrode, an FTO conductive glass film electrode was prepared for the sample to be tested as the working electrode, and the electrolyte solution was 0.1 M Na2SO4. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent curve (i-t). The preparation method for the working electrode was as follows: Five milligrams of catalyst was weighed, mixed with 0.5 mL of absolute ethanol and 10 μL of Nafion, and high-frequency ultrasound was applied for 30 min. Then, the dispersed suspension solution was uniformly coated on the conductive surface of the FTO conductive glass (the coating area was 1 cm2).

The photocatalytic performance of the prepared samples was evaluated by degrading the MB dye under visible light. A PLS-SXE 300 Xe lamp (300 W, Beijing PerfectLight Co., Ltd., China) equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as a light source for visible-light output. Fifty milligrams of catalyst were dispersed in 50 mL of MB solution with a concentration of 10 mg/L, and then the photocatalytic degradation test was carried out under magnetic stirring. Before light irradiation, the dispersion was kept in the dark under magnetic stirring for 60 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution was collected every 10 min and centrifuged to remove the catalyst, and then the absorbance at 664 nm was analyzed on a UV-2550 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer.

Through free radical capture experiments, the active species in the reaction were explored to illustrate the material that plays a role in the photocatalytic degradation of the MB system. The main content of the experiment is to add 1 mM isopropanol (IPA) as a trapping agent for hydroxyl radicals (•OH); 1 mM p-benzoquinone (BQ) is used to capture superoxide radicals (•O2 −); and 1 mM disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) is used as a reagent for capturing photogenerated holes (h+) in three independent photodegradation systems.

To confirm whether the photocatalyst has good chemical stability and reusability, the characteristics and performance of synthetic photocatalysts were explored through a cyclic degradation experiment. All the catalysts were recovered after each cycle. To remove the dye molecules adhered to the surface, the catalysts were repeatedly washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol several times, dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight, ground evenly, and then put into the next cycle system for reuse to explore their reusability. After the cycle, the used catalyst was recovered, washed and dried the same number of times and then ground evenly to be characterized by XRD and FT-IR, and the stability of the catalyst material was compared with that before use.

As shown in Scheme 1 , a simple one-pot method was used to prepare the C-dot-modified h-MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction, which has a good photocatalytic degradation effect on MB. As shown in Fig. S1–4, three methods were explored for the compounding of h-MoO3 and g-C3N4, and the compounding ratio was optimized. It can be seen from the results that regardless of which of the three methods is used, when the compounding ratio of h-MoO3 is 3 %, the MB degradation effect is the best. Among the three compounding methods, ethanol dispersion and ultrasound had the best compound effect. Second, the mixed components are calcined after ethanol is dispersed and ultrasonically agitated, and the final method is the direct mixing and calcination method. This may be due to the direct mixing and calcination method in which the h-MoO3 and g-C3N4 materials are not tightly combined, which limits electron transfer in the material. Ethanol was dispersed and mixed with ultrasound, and h-MoO3 and g-C3N4 interacted with each other to form van der Waals forces between the molecules to form a two-dimensional heterojunction, which accelerated the conduction and separation of photogenerated electrons. Since the preparation condition of h-MoO3 is 120 °C, in the calcination method after ethanol dispersion and ultrasound, although a tightly bound two-dimensional heterojunction is formed, it may be due to the destruction of the original morphology of h-MoO3. In addition, the recalcining process may cause agglomeration of the heterojunction, resulting in a certain decrease in photocatalytic activity. On the basis of the ethanol mixed ultrasonic method, the process is supplemented with a certain amount of CD modification, which may be due to the successful anchoring of the CDs to the composite material through chemical bond interactions. Such strong interactions can improve the transfer of photogenerated carriers and reduce the recombination rate of light-excited electron-hole pairs. From the degradation effect on MB, it can be seen that when the amount of CD modification is increased to 1 mL, the degradation effect of the additional amount is not much different, so the CD modification amount is selected as 1 mL. Under optimal compounding conditions, CMOCN has better photocatalytic performance than BCN. After 1 h of simulated visible light irradiation, the degradation rate of MB by CMOCN reached 67 %, which is 2.7, 2.6 and 5.8 times that of bulk g-C3N4, pure h-MoO3 and pure CDs, respectively. The degradation efficiency reached >97 % within 3 h.

Fig. S5 shows the FT-IR spectra of photocatalysts prepared with different proportions, different compounding methods, and different volume modification amounts of CDs. Fig. 3a shows the comparative FT-IR spectra of h-MoO3, BCN, MOCN-3 and 1CMOCN-3. The experiment successfully synthesized the g-C3N4-based photocatalytic material. The infrared absorption of h-MoO3 is consistent with the literature [50], indicating the successful preparation of h-MoO3. The peak between 1000 and 900 cm−1 belongs to the characteristic stretching vibration of the MoO bond of hexagonal phase MoO3 [51]. A wide and complex band appears at 600 cm−1, which corresponds to the MoO vibration of h-MoO3. The small absorption peak at 3434 cm−1 and the sharp molecular band at 1616 cm−1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibrations of the -O-H group in the water molecule. There are obvious peaks at 3200 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1, which are caused by the tensile and flexural vibrations of NH in NH4 + [52,53]. There is no obvious infrared absorption of h-MoO3 in the composite heterojunction material, which may be due to the small amount of synthesis. From SEM and TEM (Fig. S6a-b), it can be seen that the CDs are uniformly distributed. Fig. S6c shows the FT-IR spectrum of C-dots, and the characteristic absorption band of the −OH stretching vibration can be observed at approximately 3410 and 1081 cm−1. The band at 2925 cm−1 corresponds to the C − H stretching vibration. In addition, the peaks appearing at 1591 and 1390 cm−1 may be caused by the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO−, respectively [43].

Fig. S7 shows the XRD patterns of photocatalysts prepared in different proportions, with different compounding methods, and with different amounts of CD modification. The characteristic peaks of h-MoO3 and g-C3N4 appear in the composite photocatalytic material at the same time, indicating the successful composite of the two-dimensional materials. Pure h-MoO3 has four main peaks at 9.62°, 19.31°, 25.7° and 29.3°, which correspond to the (100), (200), (210) and (310) crystal planes, respectively [42]. In the three different composite methods, with an increase in h-MoO3, the characteristic peak of h-MoO3 has a certain enhancement, but due to the low content of h-MoO3 in the composite material, the absorption mode of the composite material is similar to that of pure g-C3N4. The 1CMOCN-3 composite material also showed an XRD pattern similar to that of the MOCN composite material, which may be due to the low content of CDs doped in the composite material [49,54].

Through the comparison of the XRD patterns of the heterojunction before and after CD modification (Fig. 3b), it can be seen that when CDs are used to modify the prepared h-MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction, the characteristic peak at 2θ = 13.1° shifts to a lower angle, which may be due to the interaction of h-MoO3 and g-C3N4 components in the heterojunction [55].

The specific surface areas of BCN, MOCN-3, and 1CMOCN-3 and the distribution of size and pore size in the photocatalyst were characterized and analyzed by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 4 ). It can be clearly seen that the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of BCN, MOCN-3, and 1CMOCN-3 (Fig. 4a) are all type IV isotherms [56]. The test showed that P/P0 is between 0.45 and 1, and there is an obvious hysteresis curve, indicating that there are a large number of mesopores in the three photocatalysts [57]. The specific surface area of BCN is 10.9761 m2/g and that of MOCN-3 is 12.3781 m2/g. The specific surface area of 1CMOCN is slightly higher than those of BCN and MOCN-3, at 14.5569 m2/g. This is due to the porous structure of the material after the addition of CDs, which is consistent with the SEM results. The pore size distribution diagram (Fig. 4b) shows that the heterojunction photocatalyst synthesized in this experiment is mainly mesoporous.

To further investigate the surface composition and chemical structure, XPS energy spectrum analysis was performed on the h-MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction before and after CD modification, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The full XPS spectrum of sample (Fig. 5a) shows that there are four elements, C, N, Mo, and O, on the surface of the MOCN-3 and 1CMOCN-3 catalysts. The high-resolution spectra of Mo 3d, C 1 s and N 1 s are shown in Fig. 5b-d. In the MOCN-3 sample, there are two typical peaks in the Mo 3d spectrum. The peak at 236.1 eV corresponds to the Mo 3d3/2 orbital of Mo(VI) in h-MoO3, and the peak at 232.9 eV corresponds to the Mo 3d5/2 orbital [55]. Compared with the composite material 1CMOCN-3 prepared by using CDs to modify the prepared h-MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction, the characteristic peaks of the Mo 3d3/2 orbital and the Mo 3d5/2 orbital were shifted to 235.7 eV and 232.5 eV, respectively. This may be caused by the interaction of the CDs with the h-MoO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction after the addition [50].

The light absorption performance of the photocatalyst can be obtained by measuring its ultraviolet–visible DRS spectrum (Fig. 6a) by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer, and the energy bands of BCN, MOCN-3, and 1CMOCN-3 can be obtained by plotting (Ahv)1/2 against the hv spectrum (Fig. 6b) [49,57]. It can be seen from the figure that due to the introduction of a small amount of h-MoO3, when g-C3N4 is recombined, the absorption edge of the photocatalyst is redshifted, which relatively increases the visible light absorption of bulk g-C3N4 (BCN). The photoresponse intensities of MOCN-3 and carbon-dot-modified 1CMOCN-3 are both enhanced compared with BCN, and the photoresponse intensity of 1CMOCN-3 is lower than that of MOCN-3, which may be because some sites are covered by the addition of CDs. However, compared with MOCN-3, 1CMOCN-3 has a narrower band gap energy [58], indicating that the addition of CDs improves the electron transfer ability. It is more conducive for the composite heterojunction to generate more electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation to obtain higher photocatalytic performance.

Through the cyclic degradation experiment of MB, the reusability and chemical stability of the 1CMOCN-3 photocatalyst were tested (Fig. 7a). The experimental results show that after four cycles, the material still maintains a good effect on the degradation of MB, so the experimentally synthesized 1CMOCN-3 has good reusability. The 1CMOCN-3 photocatalyst after the cyclic degradation experiment was recovered, and the degraded material was characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 7b) and SEM (Fig. 7c). As seen in the figure, the degraded photocatalytic material retains the original morphology and functional group distribution well, indicating that 1CMOCN-3 has good stability.

As shown in Table 1 , the MB degradation effect of the CMOCN material synthesized in this experiment and other g-C3N4-based photocatalysts is compared. When MB is subjected to photocatalytic degradation for 3 h, the degradation rate can reach 97 %, and the CMOCN can be seen to have a good degradation effect on MB.

The PL photoluminescence spectrum indicated the recombination efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes (Fig. S8). MOCN-3mix, MOCN-3bf, MOCN-3af and 1-CMOCN-3 all have the lowest fluorescence intensity ratio among synthetic materials under the same conditions. In contrast, with ethanol dispersion and ultrasonic mixing of h-MoO3 and g-C3N4, the composite ratio of h-MoO3 is 3 %, and the volume of CD modification is 1 mL. The material synthesized in this way has the lowest fluorescence intensity, indicating that the photocatalyst synthesized under this condition has lower photogenerated electron and hole recombination efficiency and better photocatalytic performance. This also corresponds well to the result of the photocatalytic degradation of MB.

The photocatalyst was subjected to an electrochemical impedance test under dark conditions, and the transient photocurrent response of the test material was subjected to light treatment every 20 s to explore the interface charge transfer rate of different samples. From the Nyquist diagram (Fig. 8a), it can be seen that the carbon-dot-modified g-C3N4/h-MoO3 heterojunction (1CMOCN-3) photocatalytic material is compared with the single-component g-C3N4 and h-MoO3, and the g-C3N4/h-MoO3 heterojunction (MOCN-3) has a smaller radius. The transient photocurrent response diagram (Fig. 8b) shows that when light is not applied, no significant change in the current is detected in each material, and when light is applied, the electrode detects that the current increases rapidly and is removed again. After removing the light source again, the current quickly returned to its original state. The transient current response of each material to light is 1CMOCN-3 > MOCN-3 > h-MoO3 > g-C3N4, which corresponds well to the degradation of MB and the EIS result. This may be due to the recombination of the heterojunction and the modification of the CDs greatly increasing the separation and transfer rate of the charge in the g-C3N4 material, effectively reducing the reaction recombination between the charge carriers, and the utilization rate of photogenerated electrons in the photocatalyst being further improved [59], which effectively improves the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of g-C3N4 on MB.

In addition, by comparing the luminescence intensities of 1CMOCN-3, g-C3N4, h-MoO3, and MOCN-3, the luminescence intensity of 1CMOCN-3 is lower than that of g-C3N4, h-MoO3, and MOCN-3, which indicates that the introduction of CDs can inhibit the recombination of photo-generated carriers. At the same time, the results of the transient current response under illumination show that the introduction of CDs can improve the electron lifetime and current separation efficiency [63,64]. The radius of the composite (1CMOCN-3) in the EIS is smaller, and the interface charge transfer rate is the fastest, indicating that the composite has a rapid electron transfer rate and effective carrier separation efficiency, and can effectively participate in the photocatalytic reaction [65].

Based on the UV–vis/DRS and Mott-Schotky curves, we can calculate the Eg values of SCN, CDs, and h-MoO3 to be 2.70, 3.17, and 2.78 eV, respectively; the ECB values to be −0.32, −0.37, and − 1.00; and the EVB values to be 2.43, 2.17, and 2.60. Based on this, we use Fig. 9b to represent the heterojunction and energy band structures of the three materials during the photocatalytic process, as well as the electron and hole movements in these composites under light irradiation. To explore the reaction mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction in this experiment, an experiment was carried out to capture the main active species. Fig. 9a shows the degradation curve of 1CMOCN-3 photocatalytic degradation in the MB free radical capture experiment. It can be clearly seen that when EDTA-2Na and IPA are present in the system, the degradation effect of MB is inhibited to a certain extent; when BQ is present, it has little effect on the photocatalytic degradation of MB by 1CMOCN-3. The results show that the synergistic effect of the two active species h+ and •OH in the photocatalytic process improves the catalytic activity.

In this experiment, a new type of biomass CD-modified hexagonal phase molybdenum trioxide/graphite phase carbon nitride composite photocatalytic material (1CMOCN-3) was successfully prepared, and this photocatalyst was applied to the degradation dye methylene blue (MB). By comparing the degradation effect of MB, we found the appropriate compounding method and ratio, and 1CMOCN-3 has better photocatalytic performance, reusability, and excellent stability than the original g-C3N4 and h-MoO3. Through exploratory experiments on the capture of free radicals, it was found that in the 1CMOCN-3 degradation reaction system, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and photogenerated holes (h+) are the main reactive species. This study provides a simple method for the preparation of g-C3N4 two-dimensional heterojunction materials that can be applied to the degradation of pollutants.

Dan Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Caixia Deng: Writing – review & editing. Shuang Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Xiupei Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
